In this lesson, we're going to cover asset-backed financing, which is a form of debt financing. To get started, I want to give you an overview of what debt financing is. Through debt financing, a company raises money that has to be paid back over some period of time with interest. The debt is paid regardless of company revenue, which can be risky for a smaller or newer business that doesn't yet have consistent revenue or cash flow every single month. But there are clear benefits to debt financing too, which makes it a useful tool for funding your acquisition. Unlike equity financing, which we'll cover next, you maintain full control of the business. You aren't selling ownership stakes in the business to acquire it. And most likely, the total cost of debt financing will likely or should be less than if you give away equity to fund the acquisition. With equity financing, the shares will grow with the growth of the business. It might cost you far more than the loan to buy back those shares in the future. Another benefit is that debt payments are usually tax deductible. So where exactly is this money coming from? I'm going to cover some of the different sources in this lesson. Personal Credibility Loans. One of the sources are personal credibility loans. This is a route I don't recommend, but I want to be able to explain it to you. These are loans based on personal relationships you have. This could be a mentor, a friend, a family member, or someone else close to you. The thing that's at risk is your relationship, especially with the individuals that give you money. And it's very important that you disclose the risks associated with the loan to whoever ends up lending you money. I mean, think about it, friends and family. It's kind of an awkward holiday if you don't pay them back, right? If things are going bad, it adds a whole bunch of extra pressure. This loan is based on personal trust that can cloud the vision of both you and the person lending it to you. You want to be very clear with the person so that they approach it with their eyes wide open. You don't want to give them a narrow view and ultimately damage the relationship going forward if things don't work out. We'll talk a little bit more about these loans in the future, but let's move on for now. In this lesson, I want to spend most of our time on the next source, asset backed loans. In an asset backed loan, the lender is going to be looking at the business's balance sheet. It's assets, things like receivables, fixed assets, and so on. They'll use this information to determine what the borrowing potential is. You'll want to see that the assets can cover the cost of the loan amount. And specifically, they'll be looking at assets with high liquidity, things like receivables, things that you can liquidate, sell quickly. Unlike the flexibility that a personal credibility loan has, asset backed lenders will require a certain degree of security and timelines for repayment. Some asset backed loans are revolving. Some aren't. When you borrow against something that has fluctuating value such as receivables or inventories, you have a revolving debt. This means that the loan balance may go up or down alongside the receivable and inventory in the business. In these cases, there's no expectation that the loan will ever be paid down to zero. Asset backed loans also work for equipment, machinery, or property, although with property, they aren't called revolving debt. In that case, you're getting a note or a mortgage where the principal and interest will eventually be paid down to zero. That means there are four primary categories of assets we want to examine. Land, receivables, inventory and equipment, and machinery. For asset backed loans, you're going to have to adhere to certain financial covenants required by the lender. I will go into those financial covenants in greater detail in another lesson later on, but it's important to note that these types of loans will often require some kind of covenant. Some lenders, they'll require you to provide a personal guarantee, not in all loans, and they can sometimes be avoided in an asset backed loan. Many business owners need to provide a personal guarantee even in an asset backed loan, but not all banks require it, so this is something you're going to want to negotiate. Let's move on to cash flow loans now. With these type of loans, you're often subsidizing an asset backed loan by borrowing money based on the business's current cash flow. If the assets in the business aren't enough to cover the full loan amount, a cash flow lender will provide the remainder of the funds needed. The catch here is that the business needs to have strong cash flow for this to work. If cash flow fluctuates too much or the business is losing money, it's not going to be a candidate for this option. For these types of loans, you'll often see a wide range of interest rates. They might be, say, 8% to 20% depending on who you get the loan from. There's a lot of variability there. You might also see penalties for paying the loan back too early from a lender that wants to lock in the return of the loan. There's a lot more digging to do for cash flow lenders. They look more closely than most any other lenders. As a result, they often require a certain return for that work. They don't want to just put in all that effort to only have the loan paid off in a few months. Like asset-backed loans, cash flow loans are going to have financial covenants. Again, I'll get into financial covenants in a future lesson. Just make note that cash flow loans will have stricter requirements when it comes to covenants, and it'll be more difficult to negotiate those down. It might be possible, but there's not nearly as much room for it compared to other loans. Like asset-backed lenders, cash flow lenders are likely going to request a personal guarantee on the cash flow loan. Whether this can be avoided will depend on the borrower. The more deals you go out and build a reputation on, it'll be more likely you can negotiate yourself out of guarantees in the future. In any case, cash flow loans, they're going to want to know all your liabilities, and they're going to want full disclosure, including things like seller notes. Next, we'll talk about seller notes and deferred payments. Let's start with seller notes. Sometimes a seller is willing to provide financing to a buyer for a few reasons. The seller is ready to exit the business to focus on retirement, their hobbies, start a new business, something else that they want to do, and they'll receive consideration as a result of the sale. In other words, a promise from the buyer to pay the seller later. So a seller provides the seller note. With a seller note, the seller loans money to the buyer to help finance the acquisition. But the money does not flow from the seller to the buyer and then back to the seller again. Instead, the buyer pays a certain portion of the transaction price at some later point in time. Expect seller notes to earn interest over time, and it can be paid monthly, quarterly, annually, or it could accrue over the life of the loan. In other words, they're repaid when the loan is repaid. Or accrued over the life of the loan. The fine details of the note are guided by the seller's flexibility, as well as their motivation and how badly they want to sell their business. And that in turn drives things like carrying cost and repayment timelines and all the other elements with making sure that you fund your obligation for acquiring the business. Now most sellers want something short, like one to four years. They just want to be out. And they don't want the liability that if you screw up the business, they don't get paid. So, like any other lender, there will be some covenants that you'll have to agree on. These are likely negotiable, but again, it all depends on how motivated the seller is. Let's look at how this works. Say we're looking at a software company. We'll take a look at the balance sheet, usually about three years worth, and we'll look at the most recent year starting with cash. Let's say the cash in this deal is $2 million. Now we can't borrow against the cash, but we can use it to help finance a purchase if we negotiate with the seller to leave the cash in the business. However, the surplus cash is added to the purchase price, so we do have to pay for it. So if a company has $15 million of revenue, then that $2 million is considered surplus cash. Surplus cash is usually anything above 8 to 10% of revenue. Now this number can vary. Some businesses have larger cash requirements than others. So if you take the high end of the 10% and apply it to the $15 million, we get $1.5 million in working capital. And if that's the required cash and there's $2 million in this deal, then we take the $2 million, subtract the $1.5, and we get $500,000 in cash. Now we add that surplus cash to the purchase price, as well as the closing payment. That's how we look at cash contracts receivables. Sometimes these might be called accounts receivables or trade receivables, but they mean the same thing. This is expected money to be paid by customers who have already received their goods. The customers essentially bought on credit and the amount is due to the business. These receivables are the easiest to borrow against because they're the most liquid asset. For asset-backed deals, this is the first thing we look at. And there are some limitations to them. You may not be able to finance the entire amount. The receivables need to be less than somewhere between 90 and 120 days, and there are also some other requirements as well. And the amount should ideally be spread across multiple customers. If there's just one customer or very few customers and it's concentrated, it'll be impossible or very difficult to raise financing using those receivables. Lenders want to know that there's not just a few customer invoices propping up your receivables. We'll assume 80% of the receivables is available and added to the closing payment. So 80% of the $500,000 is $400,000. So that's available to be added to the closing payment. Now we're going to look at other receivables. Let's say we're looking through the records and we find $200,000 of other receivables from several years ago. In this case, the shareholder actually borrowed money from the business. It's a liability to the shareholder and it shows up on the asset list as $200,000. It's an asset of the business, but because it's money that's been borrowed from the business by the owner, it's not an asset that we can borrow against. It's not going to play any role in the financing, so we don't need to worry about it. Now let's move on to property and equipment. Let's assume the balance sheet is showing a net amount of $3.5 million. Usually, you'll see notes explaining what the different line items actually are, and some of these line items can actually be borrowed against. Maybe we'll see notes showing five different categories. Let's say we see land, buildings, equipment, vehicles, and office furniture. The purchase price for all of this is $6 million, but there's been some depreciation. We depreciated about $3 million against the purchase price. That means the net value of the property and equipment is $3 million. That's the amount we can borrow against. It's not always as clear cut as this as I'm giving you these examples, but lenders are going to look up and down your balance sheet to determine what you can and can't use for financing. Some assets depreciate faster than other assets, so lenders won't value them the same way. Remember, there are four main categories we're looking at. We're looking at land, receivables, inventory, and equipment and machinery. For property, the equipment, let's say we use 40% of the net value. That number might even be lower in some cases. Let's use the 40% though as an example for this deal. So in this case, the 3 million net property and equipment balance is actually $1.2 million. If you're looking at inventory, it will depend on what the inventory is exactly. If the goods are perishable, you might see anywhere from a single-digit return to 20%. It all depends on the type of goods. If you see inventory listed on the balance sheet, the value might not be anywhere near what the lender would actually finance it for. And lenders will have a minimum amount that they're looking for to lend. That could be $500,000 or more. You're not going to get a lender to budge on $80,000 worth of inventory. Now let's look at land. On the balance sheet, you're going to see the land listed at the historical purchase price. If somebody bought the land for say a million dollars to build an office, the land would be valued at a million dollars on the balance sheet, regardless of what the price is now. This doesn't mean that the land can't be borrowed against at a higher value. Let's say fair market value of the land is actually 5 million. A lender will likely lend against the fair market value. Land is easy to value. It's stable and usually easy to sell. It has decent liquidity. Now you won't get 100% of the fair market value, but you might get 70% to 80%. So if the fair market value is 5 million, 80% of that is $4 million. I can tell you, I have actually seen deals where buildings were on land for the business and somebody bought the business. The owner did not know what they were doing. They sold all the assets, including the building and the land, with the business. And then the buyer went and financed the building as a tenant. It appraised out higher and it brought all the proceeds in to buy the business. So I will tell you that if you pay attention to what you're doing, you can always find these little secret nuggets of gold out there, but you have to learn how to do this. Now when you get a loan on your land, that gets put in the closing payment. So that goes to fund part of your purchase of the business. So let's look at where we're at on this deal. We have receivables of 400,000. We have property and equipment at 1.2 million and 4 million in land and about $500,000 in surplus cash. That gives us a total of 6.1 million. All of the money doesn't go to the seller because we need to keep some of it for closing costs and this will change depending on the size of the deal. In our case, let's just use $100,000 to make the remaining number 6 million that we can use for the closing payment to the seller. Now as we near the end of this lesson, I want to mention a few other things. When we're pursuing asset-back lending, the assets have to be unencumbered. That means they can't be used as security for another loan. If you're working with a balance sheet with solid asset totals, but all of those assets are already tied up for other things, then you really have a poor balance sheet. So you need to know what assets are available on your balance sheet and what can be used for asset-back financing. And when you're trying to do a leverage buyout, it becomes more difficult when you can't borrow against any of those assets because they're being leveraged for something else. The assets will have to be unencumbered and verified in order to be used. Another point to note, the lender's loan to value ratio might be different than the ratio you used. So when you model these numbers out, you don't want to be too aggressive in the ratio. You assume if you need the assets to finance the business. However, we don't want to play it too conservative either because we don't want to leave a bunch of room for negotiation. Ultimately, we're looking at three things when we're looking at debt financing. First, there have to be assets we can actually borrow against. Those assets can't be weighed down with debt. Next, we need low liability levels in the business, meaning no debt or not much debt. And finally, we need the cash flow to be strong enough to pay the interest, service the debt, and make those deferred payments. Good assets, no debt, strong cash flow. That's how we make it work. The businesses we're looking for to buy should also have a significant credit rating to secure the debt financing. The company is borrowing the money. Not us, although we'll have to probably personally guarantee it, but the company is technically borrowing the money. So if the company has terrible credit history, we're going to have a harder time securing loans. That's our lesson on asset-back financing. This is an incredibly important part of buying a business through a leveraged buyout. In the next lesson, we're going to talk about the opposite of debt financing, equity financing. I'll see you at that lesson.
